reference: GATE CS — Discrete Mathematics / Set Theory

map.condition: Checking if a relation is a POSET
Counting edges in a Hasse diagram
map.use: \text{Edges} = |\text{cover pairs}| = |\{(a,b) : a \prec b,\; \nexists c : a \prec c \prec b\}|

map.condition: Finding GLB / LUB in a divisibility poset
Finding complement of element x in a bounded lattice

formulas.name: POSET Definition (Partial Order)
formulas.latex: (A, \preceq)\text{ is a POSET iff } \preceq \text{ is Reflexive, Antisymmetric, Transitive}
formulas.plain: (A, <=) is a POSET iff <= is Reflexive, Antisymmetric, and Transitive
formulas.whenToUse: Verifying whether a given relation forms a partial order on a set
formulas.trap: Strict less-than (<) is NOT a partial order because it is irreflexive (a < a is false); it is a strict partial order

formulas.name: Reflexivity
formulas.latex: \forall a \in A,\; a \preceq a
formulas.plain: For all a in A: a <= a
formulas.whenToUse: First check when testing if a relation is a POSET
formulas.trap: Strict inequality (< or >) fails reflexivity; always distinguish <= from <

formulas.name: Antisymmetry
formulas.latex: a \preceq b \text{ and } b \preceq a \Rightarrow a = b
formulas.plain: a <= b and b <= a implies a = b
formulas.whenToUse: Second check when testing if a relation is a POSET; rules out equivalence relations
formulas.trap: Asymmetry (a<=b => NOT b<=a) is stronger than antisymmetry; strict < is asymmetric but not a POSET

formulas.name: Transitivity
formulas.latex: a \preceq b \text{ and } b \preceq c \Rightarrow a \preceq c
formulas.plain: a <= b and b <= c implies a <= c
formulas.whenToUse: Third check when testing if a relation is a POSET
formulas.trap: Only failing transitivity for some triple disqualifies the relation; check ALL triples

formulas.name: Hasse Diagram Edge Count
formulas.latex: \text{Edge } a \to b \text{ exists iff } a \prec b \text{ and } \nexists c : a \prec c \prec b
formulas.plain: Draw edge a->b iff a < b (a covers b) with no element strictly between them
formulas.whenToUse: Counting or drawing edges in a Hasse diagram from a given POSET

terms.means: a is covered by b (a < b with nothing between)

formulas.trap: Transitive edges are REMOVED in Hasse diagrams; do not draw a->c when a->b->c already exists

formulas.name: Chain, Antichain & Dilworth's Theorem
formulas.latex: \text{Dilworth: min chains needed to cover} = \text{max antichain size}
formulas.plain: Minimum number of chains to cover a POSET = size of the largest antichain (Dilworth's theorem)
formulas.whenToUse: Questions about decomposing a POSET into chains or antichains

terms.means: totally ordered subset of the POSET
subset where no two elements are comparable

formulas.trap: If the longest chain has length n, the POSET can be partitioned into n antichains (Mirsky), NOT n chains

formulas.name: Mirsky's Theorem (Chain Decomposition)
formulas.latex: \text{If longest chain} = n, \text{ then POSET} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_i\; (n\text{ antichains})
formulas.plain: A POSET with longest chain of length n can be partitioned into n antichains
formulas.whenToUse: Fill-in-the-blank questions: 'longest chain of length n => partition into ___ antichains'
formulas.trap: Mirsky gives antichain decomposition; Dilworth gives chain decomposition — do not confuse them

formulas.name: Lattice Definition
formulas.latex: (L, \preceq)\text{ is a lattice iff every pair } a,b \in L \text{ has a unique GLB } (a \wedge b) \text{ and LUB } (a \vee b)
formulas.plain: A POSET (L,<=) is a lattice iff every pair of elements has both a GLB (meet) and LUB (join)
formulas.whenToUse: Checking whether a given POSET is a lattice

terms.means: meet (greatest lower bound / infimum) of a and b
join (least upper bound / supremum) of a and b

formulas.trap: Every lattice is a POSET but NOT every POSET is a lattice; one missing GLB or LUB pair disqualifies it

formulas.name: Divisibility Lattice: GCD & LCM
formulas.latex: \text{In } (\mathbb{Z}^+, |):\quad a \wedge b = \gcd(a,b),\quad a \vee b = \text{lcm}(a,b)
formulas.plain: In the poset of positive integers ordered by divisibility: meet = gcd, join = lcm
formulas.whenToUse: Any question about the (N, divides) or ({divisors}, |) lattice — finding GLB/LUB

terms.means: a | b means a divides b

formulas.trap: GCD is the MEET (infimum), LCM is the JOIN (supremum) — students often swap these

formulas.name: Subset/Power-Set Lattice
formulas.latex: \text{In } (2^S, \subseteq):\quad A \wedge B = A \cap B,\quad A \vee B = A \cup B
formulas.plain: In the power-set lattice ordered by subset: meet = intersection, join = union
formulas.whenToUse: Questions about the lattice of subsets of a set S
formulas.trap: The subset lattice is always a distributive lattice; do not confuse it with a Boolean algebra (requires complements)

formulas.name: Absorption Laws
formulas.latex: a \vee (a \wedge b) = a \qquad a \wedge (a \vee b) = a
formulas.plain: a OR (a AND b) = a   ;   a AND (a OR b) = a
formulas.whenToUse: Simplifying lattice expressions; verifying lattice axioms
formulas.trap: Absorption holds in ALL lattices, not just distributive ones — do not mistake it as a property only of Boolean algebras

formulas.name: Distributive Lattice Laws
formulas.latex: a \wedge (b \vee c) = (a \wedge b) \vee (a \wedge c)\quad\text{and}\quad a \vee (b \wedge c) = (a \vee b) \wedge (a \vee c)
formulas.plain: a AND (b OR c) = (a AND b) OR (a AND c)  [and dual]
formulas.whenToUse: Checking if a lattice is distributive; simplifying expressions in distributive lattices
formulas.trap: It suffices to check one of the two laws — each implies the other; also, a lattice with M3 or N5 as sublattices is NOT distributive

formulas.name: Modular Lattice Law
formulas.latex: a \preceq c \Rightarrow a \vee (b \wedge c) = (a \vee b) \wedge c
formulas.plain: If a <= c, then a OR (b AND c) = (a OR b) AND c
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying if a lattice is modular but not distributive (e.g., M3 is modular but not distributive)
formulas.trap: Every distributive lattice is modular but NOT vice versa; M3 (diamond lattice) is the classic counterexample

formulas.name: M3-N5 Characterization Theorem
formulas.latex: L \text{ is distributive} \iff L \text{ contains neither } M_3 \text{ nor } N_5 \text{ as a sublattice}
formulas.plain: Lattice L is distributive iff it has no sublattice isomorphic to M3 (diamond) or N5 (pentagon)
formulas.whenToUse: Quickly determining whether a given lattice is distributive by spotting forbidden sublattices

terms.means: pentagon lattice: 0 < a < b < 1 with c incomparable to b but 0 < c < 1

formulas.trap: M3 is modular but NOT distributive; N5 is neither modular nor distributive — know both

formulas.name: Bounded Lattice
formulas.latex: \exists\, 0,1 \in L :\; \forall a \in L,\; 0 \preceq a \preceq 1
formulas.plain: A lattice is bounded if it has a least element 0 and a greatest element 1
formulas.whenToUse: Any question mentioning least/greatest element, bottom/top, or complement
formulas.trap: Every FINITE lattice is automatically bounded; infinite lattices (like Z with <=) may not be

formulas.name: Complement in a Bounded Lattice
formulas.latex: x' \text{ is a complement of } x \iff x \vee x' = 1 \text{ and } x \wedge x' = 0
formulas.plain: x' is complement of x iff x OR x' = 1 (top) AND x AND x' = 0 (bottom)
formulas.whenToUse: Finding or verifying complements of elements in a bounded lattice
formulas.trap: In a non-distributive lattice an element can have multiple complements; in a Boolean algebra complements are UNIQUE

formulas.name: Boolean Algebra / Boolean Lattice
formulas.latex: \text{Boolean algebra} \iff \text{complemented distributive lattice}
formulas.plain: A Boolean algebra is a bounded, distributive, complemented lattice
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying when a lattice is a Boolean algebra (e.g., power-set lattice, divisors of a square-free number)

terms.means: every element has at least one complement

formulas.trap: The set of divisors of n forms a Boolean algebra only when n is a product of distinct primes (square-free)

formulas.name: De Morgan's Laws in Boolean Lattice
formulas.latex: (a \vee b)' = a' \wedge b'\qquad (a \wedge b)' = a' \vee b'
formulas.plain: (a OR b)' = a' AND b'   ;   (a AND b)' = a' OR b'
formulas.whenToUse: Simplifying or proving complement identities in a Boolean algebra
formulas.trap: De Morgan's laws hold only in Boolean algebras (complemented distributive lattices), not in arbitrary lattices

formulas.name: Total Order (Linear Order / Chain)
formulas.latex: \text{Total order: } \forall a,b \in A,\; a \preceq b \text{ or } b \preceq a
formulas.plain: A total order is a POSET where every two elements are comparable
formulas.whenToUse: Distinguishing partial orders from total orders; identifying when a Hasse diagram is a single chain

terms.means: a <= b or b <= a holds

formulas.trap: Every total order is a POSET but NOT every POSET is a total order; 'partial' allows incomparable elements

formulas.name: Divisors of n as a Lattice
formulas.latex: D_n = \{d : d \mid n\},\; (D_n, |) \text{ is a distributive lattice}
formulas.plain: Set of all divisors of n under divisibility forms a distributive lattice
formulas.whenToUse: Questions giving a specific set of divisors and asking for lattice type or GLB/LUB

terms.means: set of all positive divisors of n

formulas.trap: The set {1,2,3,4,6,8,12,24} under divisibility is a distributive lattice; check if it is also a Boolean algebra (only if n is square-free)

formulas.name: Counting Chains / Antichains in Power-Set Poset
formulas.latex: |\text{max chain in } 2^{[n]}| = n+1,\quad |\text{max antichain in } 2^{[n]}| = \binom{n}{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor}
formulas.plain: In power-set of n-element set: longest chain has n+1 elements; largest antichain has C(n, floor(n/2)) elements (Dilworth/Sperner)
formulas.whenToUse: Questions about the power-set lattice asking for the size of the longest chain or widest antichain

terms.means: power set of an n-element set
Sperner number (largest level of Pascal's triangle row n)

formulas.trap: Maximum antichain is the MIDDLE level of the Boolean lattice (Sperner's theorem); many students guess n or 2^(n-1)
